nsf_profile.name: Emmanuel Agamloh

awards.title: Collaborative Research: Mixed-Pole Multi-phase Synchronous Reluctance Motor Drives
awards.fund_division: Division of Electrical, Communications and Cyber Systems

fund_divisions: Division of Electrical, Communications and Cyber Systems

papers.title: Pulsating Torque Harmonics in Electric Motors Driven by Carrier-Based PWM Multilevel Cascaded H-bridge Inverter
papers.venue: International Electric Machines and Drives Conference
papers.abstract: This paper investigates pulsating torque harmonics in the air gap of an electric motor driven by a three-phase cascaded H-bridge (CHB) multilevel inverter using single-carrier pulse width modulation (SC-PWM) techniques. SC-PWM methods are increasingly adopted in multilevel converter control due to their simplified digital implementation compared to conventional level-shifted and phase-shifted PWM approaches. However, their impact on motor drive systems, particularly air gap pulsating torque, remains largely unexplored. This study analyzes SC-PWM voltage waveforms and their influence on the air gap torque spectrum, identifying relevant electromagnetic torque harmonics using the Concordia transformation in the αβ-plane. Campbell diagrams assess potential interactions between SC-PWM-induced torsional stress and mechanical shaft resonances. Experimental validation with a 7-level CHB inverter confirms that SC-PWM without half-wave symmetry increases harmonic distortion in line-to-line voltages and phase currents, intensifying unwanted pulsating torque harmonics.

papers.title: Implementation and experimental evaluation of stator coil insulation state of health measurement techniques under SiC switching operation
papers.venue: International Electric Machines and Drives Conference
papers.abstract: This paper presents a testbed to evaluate the insulation of form-wound medium voltage stator windings. Three methods for tracking the changes to the health of the insulation are discussed in regards to their application to online monitoring of industrial machines. The methods are implemented to track changes to insulation health of form-wound coils that have undergone various levels of insulation deterioration created by thermal and electrical accelerated aging. The results of the three state of health tracking methods are presented and the methods are compared.

papers.title: Converting Induction Machines to Reluctance Machines for Industrial Motor System Efficiency Improvement
papers.venue: Annual Meeting of the IEEE Industry Applications Society
papers.abstract: This paper proposed a scalable and practical approach to modify commercially sourced induction machines into a synchronous reluctance machine to explore improving torque and efficiency. This is achieved by redesigning a new synchronous reluctance rotor of the same size to replace the original squirrel-caged rotor, while the stator is maintained without modification. The study showed that optimizing the stator of the original induction motor before conversion may yield potential benefits. The simple rotor swap approach has been shown to improve efficiency for certain prototypes without any further additional measures. Two-pole and six-pole 1kW prototype synchronous reluctance machines were developed and experimentally tested using this approach and efficiency improvement for the six-pole machine was confirmed. The approach was extended to a 5.5kW machine by conducting partial optimization of the original induction machine before the rotor replacement. The design of three different SyRM rotor geometries for this stator-optimized variant was simulated in Flux2D and average torque and torque ripple was compared.

papers.title: Comprehensive Modeling of SiC Inverter Driven Form Wound Motor Coil for Insights on Coil Insulation Stress
papers.abstract: This paper comprehensively presents an approach for modeling form wound coils of a motor driven by an inverter, with focus on the electric stresses on the coil insulation. A 10 kV SiC testbed for medium voltage form wound coils was developed to support and validate the modeling techniques discussed. A finite element analysis (FEA) model of the motor coil is presented using COMSOL 6.1. The FEA model was used to determine parameters for an electrical model based on the multi-conductor transmission line theory. The linking of these models allows for a rapid analysis of the electrical stresses the insulation can be exposed to. An experimental method for model validation using the empirical transfer function estimation (ETFE) approach to find the impedance response of the testbed for comparison to the proposed electrical model is presented and employed. The paper also uses the model to analyze the impact of insulation delamination and voids and to demonstrate the implementation of a metric called insulation state of health monitoring for both healthy and damaged coils.

papers.title: Induction Motor Machine Learning-Based Eccentricity Fault Detection Using Magnetic Flux Measurement
papers.abstract: This paper presents a data-driven methodology for detecting eccentricity faults in induction motors utilizing dual-coil flux sensing, combined with advanced signal processing for feature extraction to support the development of machine learning (ML) models. A search coil installed in a stator tooth captures high-resolution airgap flux, while a stray flux coil mounted on the stator outer frame measures leakage flux, enabling comprehensive electromagnetic signature acquisition under various operating conditions. Unlike traditional FFT-based analysis, which focuses on sideband identification, the proposed method employs a hybrid of power spectrum, spectrogram, and empirical mode decomposition to extract meaningful features that reflect the non-stationary and nonlinear characteristics of eccentric faults. These features were used to train supervised machine learning classifiers for fault identification. The method is experimentally validated on seven different operating conditions: healthy, broken rotor bar, static eccentricity, voltage unbalance, and stator winding faults (phase-to-ground, phase-to-phase, and open winding faults), achieving 100% classification accuracy on unseen test data. This high accuracy confirms the effectiveness of the proposed method in detecting eccentricity faults and demonstrates its potential for real-time condition monitoring in industrial applications.

papers.title: Induction Motor Bearing Fault Diagnosis with Machine Learning Enhanced Empirical Mode Decomposition
papers.venue: 2025 IEEE Symposium on Diagnostics for Electric Machines, Power Electronics and Drives (SDEMPED)
papers.abstract: An effective rolling bearing fault diagnostic system is paramount to avoid production downtime and costly repairs. This paper proposes a machine learning approach enhanced by empirical mode decomposition to improve bearing fault diagnostic performance under various operating conditions. The decomposition was applied to bearing vibration signals to preprocess the data into intrinsic mode functions. The Hilbert transform is used to compute the instantaneous amplitude and instantaneous frequency of these functions, thereby revealing the time spacing of impulses of the bearing defects as an effective tool for bearing fault diagnosis. The machine learning models were trained with only the crest factor and peak value of the first two decomposed intrinsic mode functions. The model's performance on five-fold cross-validation with five response classes is 98.9% for quadratic discriminant and 100% for linear support vector machine on model validation. The best performing algorithm achieved a 97.9% accuracy on an unseen vibration dataset during model testing. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method under various defect conditions.

papers.title: A Review of Stator Insulation State-of-Health Monitoring Methods
papers.abstract: Tracking the state of the health of electrical insulation in high-power electric machines has always been a topic of great interest due to the high cost of downtime associated with unexpected failures. Over the years, there have been continuous efforts to develop and improve upon methods for testing and categorizing the health and expected lifetime of stator insulation. Methods such as partial discharge, surge, and dissipation factor testing are common examples. With the increasing use of high-specific-power electric machines in new applications such as traction and wind power generation, coupled with the increasing use of wide-bandgap semiconductor device-based inverters, some traditional methods for insulation health tracking may need adjustments or be combined with newer methods to remain accurate and useful. This paper outlines a review of the traditional insulation health tracking methods and newer methods and improvements that have been proposed to address the concerns and shortcomings of traditional methods.

papers.title: A Review of Matrix Converters in Motor Drive Applications
papers.abstract: A matrix converter (MC) converts an AC source voltage into a variable-voltage variable-frequency AC output voltage (direct AC-AC) without an intermediate DC-link capacitance. By eliminating the traditional DC-link capacitor, MCs can achieve higher power densities and reliability when compared to conventional AC-DC-AC converters. MCs also offer the following characteristics: total semiconductor solution, sinusoidal input and output currents, bidirectional power flow and controllable input power factor. This paper reviews the history, recent developments and commercialization of MCs and discusses several technical requirements and challenges, including bidirectional switches, wide bandgap (WBG) opportunities using GaN and SiC, overvoltage protection, electromagnetic interference (EMI) and ride-through in motor drive applications. MC design solutions and operation are discussed, including a comparison of control and modulation techniques as well as the detailed development of space vector modulation (SVM) to provide a deep insight into the control implementation and results. The paper concludes with compelling motor drive innovation opportunities made possible by advanced MCs including fully integrated and multiphase systems. For conventional MCs, size reductions of 30% are reported, as well as efficiencies of 98% and low input current total harmonic distortion of 3–5%.

papers.title: Graphical Analysis and Control of Cascaded H-Bridge Multilevel Inverter Using Clarke Transformation With Neutral-Shift Strategy Under Nonideal Conditions
papers.venue: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
papers.abstract: This article proposes a graphical approach using a 3-D space vector representation to diagnose the unbalanced operation of a three-phase cascaded H-bridge (CHB) inverter operating under nonideal conditions. Under ideal conditions, the application of the Clarke transformation to the inverter voltage phasor results in a circular trajectory within the stationary and orthogonal ( $\alpha \beta $ )-frame, enabling the inverter to supply balanced three-phase phase/line voltages and currents to the load. In contrast, when nonideal conditions arise, such as dc voltage imbalances among the bridges or failed cells, the Clarke transformation of the inverter voltage phasor forms an elliptical path, leading to unbalanced operation. This article discusses and highlights the characteristic differences between inverter-balanced and unbalanced operation modes. Additionally, a generalized neutral-shift method has been suggested to address unequal dc voltage sources simultaneously and failed cells without differentiation. The proposed control strategy ensures the inverter maintains balanced line-to-line voltages and currents, even when its phase voltages remain unbalanced. Simulation and experimental results from a three-cell (per phase) CHB inverter with a symmetrical RL load are provided to validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach. Finally, the study explores the impact of nonideal conditions on modifying inverter voltage and current spectra.

papers.title: SiC Four-Leg Inverter for CMV and EMI Mitigation in Advanced Motor Drive Systems
papers.abstract: Wide-bandgap SiC and GaN inverters can enable high power density motor drives due to their fast-switching properties, however, their increased electromagnetic interference (EMI) and electric discharge machining (EDM) bearing currents need to be mitigated for industry applications. This paper presents a SiC four-leg inverter motor drive system with common mode voltage (CMV) elimination and with the topology of the fourth leg connected to a small inverter output differential mode filter preventing the need for a motor neutral connection and enabling widespread industrial motor drive applications. This strategic SiC topology minimizes CMV without the use of large common mode chokes, prevents damaging EDM bearing currents and enables extended motor lifetimes. Experimental validation results are presented for the three-phase four-leg inverter using four SiC $1200 ~\mathrm{V}, 84 ~\mathrm{A}$ half bridge modules with the fourth leg only connected to an optimized inverter output differential mode filter and compared with conventional three-leg operation. The experimental testbed demonstrates the efficacy of the SiC four-leg CMV elimination by meeting the MIL-STD-461G conducted emission limits and in preventing EDM bearing damage.

papers.title: Evaluation of Superconducting AC Losses in a Simplified Induction Machine Rotor Cage
papers.abstract: Superconductors offer substantial benefits over conventional conductors for use in next-generation electric machines. Under time varying magnetic fields and transport currents, superconductors experience losses known as AC losses. A research area with many open questions is AC loss of superconductors, as the loss mechanisms and evaluation methods are not well understood in literature. This paper evaluates the losses of superconducting tape stacks exposed to alternating external magnetic fields and currents. Through simulation, we discovered that stacking superconductor tapes degraded the maximum current carrying capacity of each individual tape. Given that superconductor tapes must be stacked in motor design applications, this finding is substantial. The model was experimentally and analytically verified, then extended to a simplified induction machine superconducting rotor cage, proposed in literature for the first time, and loss patterns are estimated under the influence of external magnetic fields.

papers.title: Estimation of Induction Motor Power Factor Using Machine Learning
papers.venue: International Electric Machines and Drives Conference
papers.abstract: Accurate power factor estimation is essential for optimizing the performance and efficiency of induction motors in industrial applications. This study proposes a machine learning-based model for predicting the power factor of induction motors across various power ratings, leveraging manufacturer data from sources such as ABB and Siemens. The proposed approach utilizes a comprehensive dataset to train and validate the model, ensuring high accuracy in PF estimation for motors ranging from low to high horsepower. A discussion of the machine learning algorithm, including its architecture, feature selection, and training methodology, is provided. The model’s prediction is evaluated by comparing estimated power factor values with actual data. The results underscore the potential of machine learning-driven power factor prediction in enhancing industrial energy efficiency, facilitating informed decision-making in motor selection, power quality management, and energy cost optimization.

papers.title: Partially Superconducting Induction Machine
papers.venue: International Electric Machines and Drives Conference
papers.abstract: Superconducting materials offer promising benefits over conventional conductors used in electric machines. Their high current and low loss capabilities give the potential for high power density motors. This study presents the development of a single-phase induction machine with superconductor rotor windings. An existing single-phase induction machine was modified to implement a partially superconducting machine, using high temperature superconducting tapes. Details of the rotor geometry and construction are presented. A no-load test of the machine was conducted, and it performed consistently with theory and expectations. A finite element analysis of a simplified superconducting rotor cage using the well-known H-formulation was also conducted to support the no-load test results. The machine was then tested under load, and it showed significant improvements in power output and efficiency over the unmodified baseline machine. The results of these tests are analyzed as well.

papers.title: Comparison of Single-Phase and Three-Phase Partially Superconducting Induction Machines
papers.abstract: The theory of operation of single-phase and three-phase machines differ significantly. Unlike three-phase machines, rotor losses in single-phase machines are not negligible under no-load. Consequently, a comparison of three-phase and single-phase partially superconducting induction machines presents an interesting research. A single-phase and a three-phase induction machine with superconductor rotor windings were both successfully constructed and tested in the asynchronous mode for comparison. The machines were also compared with similarly rated conventional machines. While the theoretical “synchronous” operation mode of superconducting machines could not be achieved, the results of the machines operating in the “asynchronous” mode still showed performance improvements in output power for both the single and three-phase machines compared to the conventional machines. However, high rotor currents possibly limited the potential performance gains of using superconductors, causing the superconductors to be dissipative. The three-phase machine in particular showed decreased efficiency with superconductors, due to high current draw.

papers.title: SiC Four-leg Inverter Implementing Novel CMV Elimination for Advanced Motor Drive Applications
papers.venue: International Electric Machines and Drives Conference
papers.abstract: Common mode voltage (CMV) has been known to cause EMI in a variety of power electronic inverter applications including motor drive systems where adverse effects include damaging EDM bearing currents. Wide-bandgap (WBG) inverters are being widely implemented due to their higher switching frequencies, efficiencies and power densities compared to traditional Si-based power electronics. However, a significant challenge of utilizing WBG devices in motor drive applications is the increased EMI and EDM bearing currents. This paper presents the development of a SiC four-leg inverter for advanced motor drives employing a novel CMV elimination approach to minimize conducted emissions, prevent damaging EDM bearing currents and enable extended motor lifetimes. The simulation environment and experimental testbed are presented using a Typhoon HIL as the system-level controller including verification simulation results in the Typhoon environment for different deadtimes and filtering. Experimental validation results are presented for the three-phase four-leg inverter using four SiC 1200V, 40A half bridge modules both with the fourth leg connected to the neutral of a 230/460V 5hp motor as well as with the fourth leg only connected to the small inverter output differential mode filter eliminating the need for access to the motor neutral.

papers.title: A Motor Vibration Analysis Platform Using Typhoon-HIL Simulator
papers.venue: International Electric Machines and Drives Conference
papers.abstract: Motor vibration monitoring and analyses play a critical role in predictive maintenance, enabling the efficient management of motor-drive systems to prevent unplanned downtime and operational disruptions. This paper presents a real-time hardware-in-the-loop platform for easy deployment and enhanced fault diagnosis. The proposed system integrates advanced signal processing techniques, including Fast Fourier Transform, spectrogram analysis, and empirical mode decomposition, alongside machine learning algorithms. The developed motor condition monitoring model is evaluated under various fault scenarios, including misalignment, unbalance, and bearing defects. Experimental results demonstrate that models employing certain machine learning algorithms achieved 99.8% accuracy in predicting and classifying motor fault conditions. These findings confirm the effectiveness of the proposed system, offering a low-cost, reliable solution to support real-time motor condition monitoring and predictive maintenance.

papers.title: Opportunities and Challenges of Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) Integration
papers.abstract: This paper presents an overview of the status and prospects of fuel cell electric vehicles (FC-EVs) for grid integration. In recent years, renewable energy has been explored on every front to extend the use of fossil fuels. Advanced technologies involving wind and solar energy, electric vehicles, and vehicle-to-everything (V2X) are becoming more popular for grid support. With recent developments in solid oxide fuel cell electric vehicles (SOFC-EVs), a more flexible fuel option than traditional proton-exchange membrane fuel cell electric vehicles (PEMFC-EVs), the potential for vehicle-to-grid (V2G)’s implementation is promising. Specifically, SOFC-EVs can utilize renewable biofuels or natural gas and, thus, they are not limited to pure hydrogen fuel only. This opens the opportunity for V2G’s implementation by using biofuels or readily piped natural gas at home or at charging stations. This review paper will discuss current V2G technologies and, importantly, compare battery electric vehicles (BEVs) to SOFC-EVs for V2G’s implementation and their impacts.

papers.title: High-Frequency Pulse Generator for Medium Voltage Motor Insulation Testing
papers.abstract: With the new fast-switching SiC inverters, winding insulation stresses in electric machines are expected to increase dramatically compared to excitation with silicon-based devices. To verify the capacity of a motor-drive insulation to withstand pulsed waveform excitations, the motor winding system must be tested with similar waveforms encountered during operation. A high-frequency, high-voltage pulse generator is presented to generate high-voltage repetitive pulses for testing medium voltage form wound coil insulation subjected to SiC inverter operation. The implemented system comprises a high-frequency transformer and two SiC MOSFET full bridges, generating different pulse-voltage waveforms. It is a versatile system designed to create two-level, three-level, five-level, and seven-level pulse voltage waveforms at different voltage levels, frequencies, and slew rates without additional system cost and control complexity.

papers.title: Comparison of Superconducting Joint Methods for Motor-drive Applications
papers.venue: 2024 IEEE Kansas Power and Energy Conference (KPEC)
papers.abstract: Superconducting materials offer the electrical benefits of zero resistance, high current carrying capacity, and high efficiency. These benefits will be crucial for the design of future advanced power electronic motor-drives and electric machines for emerging applications such as electrified aviation. Despite these benefits, there are significant challenges associated with working with these materials. One such difficulty is the task of joining superconductors together, and the focus of this study is the evaluation of different methods for joining superconductors for these emerging applications. The objective of this evaluation is to discover the superconducting joint method with the least electrical resistance. Several joints were constructed with superconducting materials using different fabrication techniques such as soldering and epoxy. The resistances of each joint type were measured and compared, using different measurement techniques with varied precision, including the well-known four-probe method. The study found that SnPb solder reasonably preserved the low resistance quality of superconductors, and performed consistently across multiple iterations, and therefore appears to be the best method to use in practical applications.

papers.title: Modeling and experimental analysis of SiC-driven form wound coil insulation with defects
papers.abstract: This paper presents a novel approach for modeling the electric stresses on the insulation of inverter driven form wound coils with a focus on the application of SiC devices. A 10kV SiC testbed for form wound medium voltage coils was developed to investigate the impact of defects within the insulation and insulation breakdown. The testbed was then modeled using finite element analysis and analytical tools based on the multi-conductor transmission line theory. A methodology for modeling the impact of conductor voltages, including the overvoltage phenomenon, was implemented. The model is validated using the impedance response of the test coils for both healthy coils and coils machined to imitate groundwall delaminations. Following this the impact of delaminations and voids on the electrical stress of the coil insulation is presented.

papers.title: Review of Fuel-Cell Electric Vehicles
papers.abstract: This paper presents an overview of the status and future prospects of fuel-cell electric vehicles (FC-EVs). As global concerns about emissions escalate, FC-EVs have emerged as a promising substitute for traditional internal combustion engine vehicles. This paper discusses the fundamentals of fuel-cell technology considering the major types of fuel cells that have been researched and delves into the most suitable fuel cells for FC-EV applications, including comparisons with mainstream vehicle technologies. The present state of FC-EVs, ongoing research, and the challenges and opportunities that need to be accounted for are discussed. Furthermore, the comparison between promising proton-exchange membrane fuel cell (PEMFC) and solid oxide fuel cell (SOFC) technologies used in EVs provides valuable insights into their respective strengths and challenges. By synthesizing these aspects, the paper aims to provide a comprehensive understanding and facilitate decision-making for future advancements in sustainable FC-EV transportation, thereby contributing to the realization of a cleaner, greener, and more environmentally friendly future.

papers.title: Comparison of Phase-Shifted and Phase-Disposition PWM Schemes in an Asymmetrical Cascaded H-Bridge Converter
papers.abstract: This paper presents a detailed and comprehensive performance comparison between phase-disposition (PD) and phase-shifted (PS) pulse-width-modulation (PWM) schemes. The comparison is carried out in a single-phase, two-cell asymmetrical cascaded H-bridge (ACHB) converter that uses DC-voltage sources chosen according to a geometric progression with a factor of two and three. The converter controlled by each PWM method is designed to generate an output voltage waveform with 7 and 9 levels under 2:1 and 3:1 DC voltage ratios, respectively. The paper analyzes the effects of PWM-induced harmonic components of each method on the converter’s output current and voltage waveforms. The comparison is conducted on Typhoon HIL-based real-time simulation and lab prototype experimental results at different operating conditions. The comparative performance includes the spectra and THDs of the converter output voltages and currents, as well as the electromagnetic interference (EMI) noise emission. The single-phase ACHB demonstrates improved harmonic performance and 56% lower THD values when utilizing the PS-PWM technique compared to the PD-PWM method. The PS-PWM method operates at 6 times higher frequency than the PD-PWM method, leading to high switching losses, EMI noise emission, and lower efficiency. In contrast, the PD-PWM method lowers switch stress and reduces switching losses as less heat is generated.

papers.title: A Review of Bidirectional Charging Grid Support Applications and Battery Degradation Considerations
papers.abstract: Electric vehicles (EVs) are crucial in mitigating global emissions by replacing internal combustion engines. The capacity of EV batteries, coupled with their charging infrastructure, offers the added advantage of supplying flexible demand capacity and providing demand response benefits to the power grid, which is essential as overall demand increases. EVs ready for vehicle-to-everything (V2X) applications and chargers that support them enhance this flexibility by allowing for varied storage applications. However, to fully harness these benefits, it is vital to consider EV drivers’ charging habits and optimize the charging and discharging controls to minimize battery life impact. This study examines various V2X applications in North America and their effects on battery longevity, considering EV charging patterns. Additionally, it investigates advanced aging-aware optimization algorithms for managing bidirectional charging.

papers.title: Superconductor Joint Methods and AC Losses in Electric Machine Applications
papers.abstract: Superconducting materials offer promising benefits for electric machine applications, as their low loss characteristics can provide improved performance over traditional conductors. However, joining superconductors poses a challenge, as joints need to preserve superconductivity and their low loss benefits. This study presents a comparison of superconducting joint methods. The most promising joint method was applied to evaluate alternating current losses of superconductors, subjected to transport currents and external magnetic fields. The study also presents the fabrication and preliminary testing of a single-phase superconducting induction machine.

papers.title: Inter-bridge Power-Sharing Capabilities in a Single-Phase 5-level Cascaded H-Bridge Inverter
papers.abstract: This paper investigates inter-bridge power-sharing capabilities for a 5-level cascaded H-bridge inverter (CHBI) operation using different redundant state combinations with the conventional phase-disposition pulse-width-modulation (PD-PWM) technique. It is demonstrated that redundant switching states can be chosen to distribute the power among different modules evenly or unevenly (50% imbalance is possible). As a result, the desired power distribution between the modules with the conventional PD-PWM method is achievable by choosing appropriate redundant switching states without tuning the modulation stage. The proposed method combines balanced power and switching loss distribution across all modulation indices in a single fundamental period. Theoretical analyses of the power balance capability of a 5-level CHBI under the conventional PD-PWM method, followed by PLECS-based simulation results, are presented to demonstrate the advantages of the proposed switching method. Finally, experimental results were provided from a 5-level SiC MOSFET-based CHB inverter Lab setup controlled by a Typhoon HIL 402 device to validate the balance power-sharing method.

papers.title: Development of Novel Conductive Grease to Mitigate PWM Inverter-Induced Bearing Currents
papers.abstract: Common mode voltages generated by high frequency, high dv/dt switching of inverters cause shaft voltages and bearing currents in motor-drive systems. When the shaft voltage magnitude exceeds the dielectric insulating capability of the bearing lubricant, a damaging electric discharge machining (EDM) current pulses through the bearing, leading to eventual bearing failure. The integration of wide band gap switching devices such as silicon carbide (SiC) in the motor drive exacerbates shaft voltage and EDM bearing current application issues. the This paper presents a novel conductive bearing grease that was developed to eliminate EDM damage and extend bearing life in SiC inverter driven motors. Shaft voltages and bearing current discharge energies associated with the novel grease are examined along with the measured conductive grease bearing capacitance and the grease consistency. The shaft voltages, bearing currents, and bearing current discharge energies present in the SiC inverter driven induction motor with conductive grease bearings are characterized, the rheological properties of the novel conductive grease are examined and compared to that of conventional bearing grease, and the lifetime of the conductive grease bearings are predicted.

papers.title: Characterization and Analysis of Bearing Currents and Shaft Voltages in SiC Inverter Driven PM Machine
papers.abstract: The high frequency voltage components generated by pulse width modulated voltages from a variable frequency drive cause voltage build up on the shaft of the inverter-driven machines resulting in bearing damage. Prior work has been done to characterize this phenomenon with several mitigation measures proposed for induction machines. Because the use of permanent magnet (PM) machines is increasing in various applications there are significant research gaps on characterizing shaft voltages and bearing currents in PM machines and their effects on PM machine bearings. This paper presents an investigation of the shaft voltages and bearing currents of PM motors operated with silicon (Si) and silicon carbide (SiC) inverter drives. This paper will characterize, compare, and analyze the differences between the resulting shaft voltage and bearing current of Si and SiC inverters driving induction and PM machines under varying load and temperature conditions.

papers.title: A Review of Offshore Renewable Energy for Advancing the Clean Energy Transition
